clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman underwent large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ) for CIN 3. Histology confirms complete excision with clear margins. She is now at her 6-month post-treatment follow-up visit. Repeat co-test (cytology + HPV) is performed. Her HPV result returns positive for high-risk HPV but cytology is negative (normal). What is the most appropriate next step in her management?
options: Discharge back to routine 3-yearly cervical screening
Repeat co-test in 12 months
Refer immediately for repeat colposcopy and biopsy
Perform repeat LLETZ without further investigation
Reassure and discharge as HPV positivity is expected post-treatment
explanation: After LLETZ for CIN 3, test of cure requires a negative co-test (HPV negative AND normal cytology). Persistent high-risk HPV positivity at 6 months, even with negative cytology, indicates incomplete viral clearance and residual risk; the correct step is to repeat co-test in 12 months rather than immediate colposcopy or discharge. Immediate colposcopy is reserved for cytological abnormality or persistence beyond 24 months. Discharging the patient would miss ongoing viral activity and risk of recurrent CIN.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Discharge is inappropriate; test of cure requires HPV-negative result before returning to routine screening.
Correct — persistent HPV with negative cytology at 6 months warrants a repeat co-test at 12 months per post-treatment surveillance guidelines.
Immediate colposcopy is indicated if cytology is also abnormal, or if HPV positivity persists beyond 24 months; not at 6 months with negative cytology alone.
Repeat LLETZ without histological evidence of recurrence is over-treatment and not guideline-supported.
HPV positivity post-LLETZ is a marker of incomplete eradication and carries ongoing risk; it should not be dismissed.
clinicalPearl: Test of cure after LLETZ requires BOTH HPV negativity AND normal cytology; HPV positivity alone with negative cytology mandates continued surveillance, not discharge.
keyClue: HPV positive but cytology negative at 6-month post-LLETZ follow-up
commonExamTrap: Assuming negative cytology alone is sufficient for discharge after treatment of high-grade CIN.
missedConcept: Post-treatment test of cure requires dual negativity; isolated HPV persistence signals ongoing risk even without cytological atypia.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman is referred for colposcopy following a smear result showing high-grade dyskaryosis (severe). She has no symptoms. On colposcopic examination, aceto-white epithelium with a coarse mosaic pattern and irregular punctation is seen at the transformation zone. The iodine (Schiller) test reveals a large iodine-negative (mustard-yellow) area. A directed punch biopsy is taken. Which colposcopic finding is the strongest predictor of underlying CIN 3 in this scenario?
options: Aceto-white change alone in the outer transformation zone
Fine punctation within the inner transformation zone
Coarse mosaic pattern with irregular punctation at the transformation zone
A nabothian follicle with surrounding erythema
Iodine-positive (brown) uptake in the squamocolumnar junction
explanation: High-grade CIN (CIN 2-3) is characterised colposcopically by dense aceto-white epithelium, coarse mosaic and coarse punctation, irregular vessel patterns, and sharp margins near the squamocolumnar junction. Coarse mosaic with irregular punctation represents disordered microvasculature and is a hallmark of high-grade disease. Fine punctation and thin aceto-white change are more consistent with low-grade lesions or metaplasia. Nabothian follicles are benign retention cysts. Iodine positivity (brown) indicates glycogenated, normal epithelium.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thin aceto-white change in the outer transformation zone is more suggestive of low-grade CIN or immature metaplasia, not CIN 3.
Fine punctation is a low-grade colposcopic feature and does not predict CIN 3.
Correct — coarse mosaic and irregular punctation near the transformation zone are high-grade colposcopic features strongly associated with CIN 3.
Nabothian follicles with erythema are benign; erythema alone may reflect inflammation, not CIN.
Iodine uptake (positive/brown) indicates normal glycogenated squamous epithelium; CIN lesions are iodine-negative (yellow).
clinicalPearl: Coarse mosaic, coarse punctation, and dense aceto-white epithelium with sharp borders are high-grade colposcopic features that predict CIN 2/3 and mandate biopsy.
keyClue: Coarse mosaic pattern with irregular punctation at the transformation zone
commonExamTrap: Assuming all aceto-white change indicates high-grade disease — thin, feathery aceto-white change is a low-grade feature.
missedConcept: Colposcopic grading relies on vessel calibre, pattern regularity, and aceto-white density — coarse patterns indicate high-grade CIN.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman attends her first cervical screening appointment. She received the bivalent HPV vaccine (HPV 16 and 18) as part of the national programme at age 12. Her primary HPV test returns positive for a non-16/18 high-risk HPV type (HPV 31), with mildly abnormal cytology (borderline nuclear changes). She asks about her prognosis compared with an unvaccinated peer with the same result. Which statement most accurately reflects her situation?
options: Her HPV vaccine provides cross-protection against HPV 31, so her risk of CIN 3 is negligible and she can return to routine screening
Her vaccine confers no benefit against HPV 31; her risk of progression to CIN 3+ is equivalent to an unvaccinated woman with HPV 31
She should be reassured that vaccinated women never develop CIN 3 regardless of HPV type
Vaccination against HPV 16/18 provides partial cross-protective immunity against HPV 31, reducing but not eliminating her risk of high-grade CIN
She requires immediate LLETZ as vaccinated women with any high-risk HPV are at higher risk of rapid progression
explanation: The bivalent (Cervarix) and nonavalent vaccines provide partial cross-protective immunity against phylogenetically related high-risk HPV types, including HPV 31 and 33. Clinical trials (PATRICIA, CVT) demonstrated approximately 70-90% cross-protection against persistent HPV 31 infection and associated CIN 2/3. Therefore, a vaccinated woman with HPV 31 has a reduced — but not eliminated — risk of high-grade CIN compared with an unvaccinated peer. She still requires standard management per her co-test result (referral to colposcopy for high-risk HPV with abnormal cytology).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cross-protection against HPV 31 is real but partial, not complete; the risk is reduced, not negligible, and she still needs appropriate surveillance.
This underestimates the cross-protective benefit demonstrated in large RCTs; her risk is lower than an unvaccinated peer.
Vaccination does not guarantee zero risk of CIN 3; other high-risk HPV types not covered by the vaccine can still cause high-grade disease.
Correct — partial cross-protection against HPV 31 is a documented benefit of HPV 16/18 vaccines, reducing but not eliminating CIN 3 risk.
There is no evidence that vaccination increases progression risk; this is clinically incorrect and would cause unnecessary harm.
clinicalPearl: HPV 16/18 vaccines provide partial cross-protection against closely related oncogenic types (HPV 31, 33, 45), reducing but not eliminating high-grade CIN risk — vaccinated women still require cervical screening.
keyClue: Bivalent vaccine recipient with HPV 31 positivity — cross-protection is partial, not complete
commonExamTrap: Assuming vaccination against HPV 16/18 provides no cross-protection against other high-risk types.
missedConcept: Cross-protective immunity from HPV vaccines is phylogenetically mediated and reduces but does not abolish risk from non-vaccine HPV types.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman at 28 weeks' gestation is reviewed in the antenatal clinic. She reports mild exertional dyspnoea and occasional palpitations. On examination, her pulse is 92 bpm, blood pressure 108/68 mmHg, and a soft ejection systolic murmur grade 2/6 is audible at the left sternal edge. ECG shows a slightly leftward axis shift. Her haemoglobin is 105 g/L. Echocardiogram shows no structural abnormality. Which underlying physiological mechanism best explains the combination of her symptoms and examination findings?
options: Decreased cardiac output due to uterine compression of the inferior vena cava
Increased plasma volume disproportionate to red cell mass expansion, with reduced systemic vascular resistance
Progesterone-induced bronchoconstriction causing reduced tidal volume
Increased red cell mass causing hyperviscosity and turbulent flow
Elevated thyroid hormone levels causing a hypermetabolic state in pregnancy
explanation: In normal pregnancy, plasma volume expands by approximately 50% while red cell mass expands by only 25%, producing a dilutional (physiological) anaemia. Simultaneously, systemic vascular resistance falls due to progesterone and prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation, lowering blood pressure. Cardiac output rises by 40-50%, causing increased stroke volume and heart rate — hence palpitations and an ejection systolic flow murmur. These are entirely physiological. The leftward ECG axis shift results from diaphragmatic elevation by the gravid uterus displacing the heart.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IVC compression in the supine position transiently reduces venous return and cardiac output, but this does not explain the murmur, dilutional anaemia, or resting tachycardia in a standing patient at clinic.
Correct — disproportionate plasma expansion relative to red cell mass causes dilutional anaemia; reduced SVR and increased cardiac output explain the murmur, tachycardia, and low blood pressure.
Progesterone stimulates the respiratory centre, increasing tidal volume and causing a mild respiratory alkalosis; it does not cause bronchoconstriction.
Red cell mass actually fails to keep pace with plasma volume in normal pregnancy, resulting in lower haematocrit, not hyperviscosity.
Although thyroid hormone levels change in pregnancy, a hypermetabolic state causing these findings is not the primary physiological explanation; thyrotoxicosis would show a different profile.
clinicalPearl: Physiological anaemia of pregnancy results from plasma volume expanding (50%) more than red cell mass (25%); the associated high-output state explains the flow murmur, sinus tachycardia, and low blood pressure.
keyClue: Normal echocardiogram with dilutional anaemia, systolic flow murmur, and low BP at 28 weeks
commonExamTrap: Treating a grade 2 systolic murmur in pregnancy as pathological — flow murmurs are physiological in high-output states.
missedConcept: The physiological haemodynamic changes of pregnancy (increased plasma volume, reduced SVR, raised cardiac output) mimic but are distinct from pathological cardiovascular disease.

subtopic: Urinary tract dilation and infection risk
clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman at 22 weeks' gestation presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of right-sided loin pain, fever (38.8°C), rigors, and dysuria. Urinalysis shows leucocytes, nitrites, and blood. Urine culture grows Escherichia coli >10^5 CFU/mL. Ultrasound reveals mild right-sided hydronephrosis. Her blood pressure is normal and fetal movements are reported as normal. She is started on IV cefuroxime. Beyond treating the acute infection, which complication most specifically associated with the physiological changes of pregnancy is she at greatest risk of developing if the infection is not adequately treated?
options: Chronic pyelonephritis progressing to end-stage renal disease
Preterm labour and delivery
Gestational diabetes mellitus
Nephrotic syndrome
Autoimmune glomerulonephritis
explanation: Physiological hydronephrosis of pregnancy (predominantly right-sided due to uterine dextrorotation and compression of the right ureter by the ovarian vein) predisposes to ascending urinary tract infection and pyelonephritis. Untreated or inadequately treated pyelonephritis in pregnancy is strongly associated with preterm labour, as bacterial endotoxins and the inflammatory cytokine cascade stimulate uterine prostaglandin production and myometrial contractility. This is a well-established and specific obstetric complication. Chronic renal disease is a long-term risk but not the most immediate obstetric concern.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While severe recurrent pyelonephritis can impair renal function, this is a remote long-term complication; the immediate, pregnancy-specific risk is preterm labour.
Correct — pyelonephritis triggers prostaglandin-mediated uterine contractions, making preterm labour the most important pregnancy-specific complication of inadequately treated upper UTI.
Gestational diabetes has a metabolic aetiology unrelated to renal infection; it is not a complication of pyelonephritis.
Nephrotic syndrome arises from glomerular pathology; it is not a complication of ascending pyelonephritis in an otherwise healthy kidney.
Autoimmune glomerulonephritis is not provoked by a bacterial upper UTI in the context described.
clinicalPearl: Pyelonephritis in pregnancy is facilitated by physiological hydronephrosis and ureteric dilation; it must be treated aggressively because bacterial endotoxins trigger preterm labour via prostaglandin release.
keyClue: Right-sided pyelonephritis at 22 weeks with physiological hydronephrosis — the specific obstetric risk is preterm labour
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on renal sequelae and missing preterm labour as the most critical obstetric complication.
missedConcept: Physiological ureteric dilation in pregnancy creates a reservoir for ascending infection; pyelonephritis is a significant cause of preterm labour.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman at 30 weeks' gestation is reviewed after routine blood gases taken during an antenatal visit show: pH 7.44, PaCO2 30 mmHg, PaO2 102 mmHg, HCO3- 20 mmol/L. She is asymptomatic with no breathlessness at rest, no chest pain, and normal oxygen saturations. She asks why her blood CO2 is low. What is the correct explanation for this blood gas pattern?
options: Pathological hyperventilation caused by pregnancy-induced anxiety disorder
Compensated metabolic acidosis from placental lactate accumulation
Physiological progesterone-driven increase in minute ventilation reducing PaCO2, with compensatory renal bicarbonate excretion
Reduced haemoglobin buffering capacity due to dilutional anaemia causing a primary metabolic alkalosis
Fetal carbon dioxide transfer across the placenta causing maternal hypocapnia
explanation: Progesterone acts as a direct respiratory stimulant, sensitising the medullary respiratory centre to CO2 and increasing tidal volume (not primarily rate) — resulting in increased minute ventilation. This produces a chronic respiratory alkalosis (low PaCO2, high pH). To maintain acid-base homeostasis, the kidneys compensate by excreting bicarbonate, resulting in the characteristically low HCO3- of pregnancy (normally 18-22 mmol/L). The net result is a mild compensated respiratory alkalosis that is entirely physiological. This pattern is identical to what is seen here and must not be misinterpreted as pathological hyperventilation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anxiety-driven hyperventilation is acute and symptomatic; this woman is asymptomatic at 30 weeks — the pattern is a predictable physiological adaptation.
Compensated metabolic acidosis would show a low pH or normal pH with low HCO3- as primary event and low PaCO2 as compensation; here the respiratory alkalosis is the primary change.
Correct — progesterone raises minute ventilation, lowering PaCO2; kidneys compensate by excreting HCO3-, yielding the physiological mild compensated respiratory alkalosis of pregnancy.
Dilutional anaemia may slightly reduce buffering capacity but does not cause a primary metabolic alkalosis; bicarbonate is low, not high, here.

Fetal CO2 does cross the placenta, but the net direction favours fetal to maternal transfer (CO2 follows gradient from high fetal to lower maternal); the primary driver of maternal hypocapnia is progesterone-mediated ventilation, not passive fetal transfer.
clinicalPearl: Pregnancy produces a physiological compensated respiratory alkalosis: progesterone drives hyperventilation → low PaCO2 → renal HCO3- excretion → HCO3- ~20 mmol/L. This must not be misread as pathological.
keyClue: Asymptomatic woman at 30 weeks with low PaCO2, low HCO3-, slightly alkaline pH — classic physiological respiratory alkalosis of pregnancy
commonExamTrap: Labelling the normal low PaCO2 and HCO3- of pregnancy as pathological hyperventilation or metabolic acidosis.
missedConcept: Progesterone-mediated hyperventilation and compensatory renal bicarbonate excretion are the physiological basis of the respiratory alkalosis of normal pregnancy.

clinicalStem: A 36-year-old woman at 34 weeks' gestation is brought to the labour ward after a road traffic accident. She is haemodynamically stable on arrival but develops severe abdominal pain and a woody-hard uterus. Fetal heart rate monitoring shows late decelerations. She is taken to theatre for emergency caesarean section. Immediately post-delivery, profuse oozing is noted from the uterine incision and IV access sites. Bloods show: platelets 58 x10^9/L, fibrinogen 0.9 g/L, PT 22 seconds (control 12), APTT 55 seconds (control 30), D-dimer markedly elevated. What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?
options: Administer low molecular weight heparin to prevent further clot consumption
Transfuse packed red blood cells alone and await coagulation normalisation
Initiate massive haemorrhage protocol: fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate, platelets, and packed red cells in a balanced ratio
Administer vitamin K intravenously and repeat coagulation tests in 4 hours
Perform hysterectomy immediately without blood product replacement
explanation: The clinical picture is DIC secondary to placental abruption: consumptive coagulopathy with thrombocytopenia, hypofibrinogenaemia (fibrinogen <1.5 g/L is critically low), prolonged clotting times, and elevated D-dimer. Immediate management requires activation of the massive haemorrhage protocol with balanced component therapy: FFP to replace clotting factors, cryoprecipitate to specifically replace fibrinogen (target >2 g/L), platelets (target >50 x10^9/L during active bleeding), and packed red cells for oxygen-carrying capacity. Heparin is contraindicated in obstetric DIC. Vitamin K has no role in acute DIC. Blood alone without coagulation factors will worsen the coagulopathy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Heparin is contraindicated in obstetric DIC; it may worsen bleeding by inhibiting residual thrombin formation needed for haemostasis.
Transfusing red cells alone dilutes already depleted clotting factors and worsens coagulopathy; component therapy in a balanced ratio is essential.
Correct — massive haemorrhage protocol with FFP, cryoprecipitate (to raise fibrinogen), platelets, and RBC in balanced ratio is the cornerstone of DIC management in obstetric haemorrhage.
Vitamin K corrects vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency (e.g., warfarin reversal) but has no role in the consumptive coagulopathy of DIC; effect would also take hours.
Hysterectomy may ultimately be required for uterine atony, but initiating blood product replacement to correct coagulopathy is the immediate priority before any surgical decision.
clinicalPearl: Obstetric DIC with fibrinogen <1.5 g/L requires immediate cryoprecipitate and FFP; fibrinogen is the first coagulation factor to fall and the best marker of DIC severity in pregnancy.
keyClue: Fibrinogen 0.9 g/L, thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/APTT, and bleeding from wounds post-abruption — DIC requiring balanced component therapy
commonExamTrap: Giving only red cells or attempting surgical haemostasis without first correcting the underlying coagulopathy.
missedConcept: In obstetric DIC, fibrinogen is a critical early marker and cryoprecipitate is the targeted treatment; heparin is contraindicated.
redFlagReason: Active DIC with life-threatening haemorrhage and hypofibrinogenaemia post-placental abruption requires immediate massive haemorrhage protocol activation to prevent maternal death.

subtopic: Gestational vs ITP vs HELLP differential
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks' gestation is referred to the day assessment unit with an incidental platelet count of 78 x10^9/L found on routine bloods. She has no symptoms. Blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg. Urinalysis is negative for protein. LFTs, LDH, urea, and creatinine are all within normal limits. She reports no history of easy bruising or bleeding prior to pregnancy. Her platelet count was 210 x10^9/L at booking (12 weeks). She is not on any medications. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: HELLP syndrome
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
Gestational thrombocytopenia
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Pre-eclampsia with thrombocytopenia
explanation: Gestational thrombocytopenia (GT) is the most common cause of thrombocytopenia in pregnancy (~75% of cases). It characteristically presents in the third trimester, is asymptomatic, does not fall below 70 x10^9/L in most cases, is not associated with neonatal thrombocytopenia, and resolves spontaneously postpartum. The absence of hypertension, proteinuria, elevated LFTs/LDH (ruling out HELLP and pre-eclampsia), and the lack of pre-pregnancy thrombocytopenic history (arguing against ITP) make GT the most likely diagnosis. TTP would present with microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, renal failure, and neurological features.
wrongAnswerExplanations: HELLP requires elevated liver enzymes, elevated LDH, and low platelets alongside haemolysis; LFTs and LDH are normal here, making HELLP unlikely.

ITP can occur in pregnancy but typically has a pre-existing or earlier-onset history; the platelet count was normal at booking (210) and the patient has no prior bleeding history, making GT more likely.
Correct — gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause, presenting asymptomatically in the third trimester with mild-moderate thrombocytopenia and no associated organ dysfunction.
TTP would manifest with haemolytic anaemia, neurological symptoms, fever, and renal impairment — none of which are present here.
Pre-eclampsia requires hypertension (≥140/90) and/or proteinuria; both are absent, making this diagnosis untenable.
clinicalPearl: Gestational thrombocytopenia is benign, asymptomatic, third-trimester onset, resolves postpartum, and requires distinguishing from HELLP (elevated LFTs/LDH) and ITP (pre-pregnancy history) which carry management implications.
keyClue: Asymptomatic, isolated mild thrombocytopenia at 36 weeks with normal BP, LFTs, LDH, and normal booking platelet count
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing HELLP or ITP without checking whether liver enzymes and LDH are elevated — the key distinguishing lab findings.
missedConcept: Gestational thrombocytopenia is the default diagnosis for mild asymptomatic thrombocytopenia in the third trimester when HELLP, ITP, and TTP have been excluded.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman is referred to the recurrent miscarriage clinic after three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages. Investigations reveal persistently positive anticardiolipin antibodies (IgG, >40 GPL units) on two occasions 12 weeks apart, and a positive lupus anticoagulant test. She has no other medical history, is not on any medication, and has never had a thrombotic event. She is not currently pregnant. What is the most appropriate management for her next pregnancy to reduce the risk of further pregnancy loss?
options: Reassure and no pharmacological intervention, as most miscarriages are chromosomal
Low-dose aspirin alone from the time of positive pregnancy test
Low molecular weight heparin alone from the time of positive pregnancy test
Low-dose aspirin commenced pre-conception combined with prophylactic dose LMWH from the time of positive pregnancy test
Full anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin throughout the first trimester only
explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) defined by obstetric criteria (three or more miscarriages) plus persistently positive antiphospholipid antibodies is an established treatable cause of recurrent pregnancy loss. The standard management to improve live birth rates is low-dose aspirin (75-150 mg/day, started pre-conception or at confirmation of pregnancy) combined with prophylactic-dose LMWH from a positive pregnancy test. This combination reduces recurrence risk significantly (from ~90% to ~50-75% live birth rate in treated women). Aspirin alone or LMWH alone is inferior to combination therapy. Full anticoagulation is reserved for women with prior thrombotic events.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While chromosomal causes account for sporadic miscarriages, three consecutive losses with confirmed APS require treatment; reassurance alone is inappropriate and harmful.
Aspirin alone is insufficient for obstetric APS; combination with LMWH provides superior outcomes as demonstrated in RCOG and international guidelines.
LMWH alone without aspirin is not the standard of care; the evidence base and guidelines support combination therapy for obstetric APS.

Correct — low-dose aspirin pre-conception plus prophylactic LMWH from a positive pregnancy test is the guideline-recommended combination treatment for obstetric APS to prevent recurrent pregnancy loss.
Full anticoagulation doses are reserved for women with prior thrombotic APS; prophylactic LMWH is appropriate for purely obstetric APS without prior thrombosis.
clinicalPearl: Obstetric APS (recurrent miscarriage + persistently positive antiphospholipid antibodies) is treated with aspirin pre-conception + prophylactic LMWH from a positive pregnancy test — combination therapy significantly improves live birth rates.
keyClue: Three consecutive miscarriages with persistently positive lupus anticoagulant and anticardiolipin antibodies — antiphospholipid syndrome
commonExamTrap: Using aspirin alone or full anticoagulation instead of the aspirin + prophylactic LMWH combination recommended for obstetric APS.
missedConcept: APS is the most important treatable cause of recurrent miscarriage; combination aspirin and LMWH is evidence-based standard of care.

topic: Cord prolapse — recognition and immediate management
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old multigravida at 39 weeks' gestation presents to the labour ward after her membranes spontaneously ruptured at home. On vaginal examination the midwife palpates a loop of pulsating umbilical cord below the presenting part in the vagina. The CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with deep variable decelerations. She is 3 cm dilated, the presenting part is a cephalic at -2 station, and the cervix is not fully dilated. What is the single most critical immediate action?
options: Administer 500 mcg IV terbutaline and prepare for immediate caesarean section without any positional change
Place the woman in the knee-chest position and manually elevate the presenting part off the cord while summoning the obstetric team for emergency caesarean section
Attempt to replace the cord into the uterus through the cervix
Perform immediate operative vaginal delivery (forceps) at 3 cm dilatation
Apply continuous pressure to the cord to stimulate circulation until theatre is ready
explanation: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate action to prevent fetal asphyxia. The life-saving immediate measure is to relieve compression of the cord by manually displacing the presenting part upward (via a hand in the vagina) while simultaneously placing the woman in the knee-chest or left lateral Trendelenburg position — this reduces cord compression from the fetal presenting part. This buys time for emergency caesarean section to be organised. The cord must not be compressed or replaced (attempting repositioning risks vasospasm). Tocolysis with terbutaline may be used as an adjunct but is not the primary first step. Operative vaginal delivery requires full dilatation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Tocolysis (terbutaline) can reduce uterine contractions compressing the cord and is a useful adjunct, but the most immediate critical action is manually elevating the presenting part off the cord — this must not be delayed.
Correct — immediate manual elevation of the presenting part combined with knee-chest position is the primary life-saving intervention for cord prolapse, buying time for emergency caesarean section.
Replacing the cord into the uterus is not recommended as it risks vasospasm and further cord damage; the cord should be kept warm and moist but not manipulated back inside.
Operative vaginal delivery is indicated only at full dilatation; at 3 cm it is impossible and would cause catastrophic injury.
Applying pressure to the cord to stimulate circulation is incorrect and harmful; compression of the cord is the very mechanism of asphyxia that must be relieved.
clinicalPearl: Cord prolapse: immediately elevate the presenting part off the cord (manual or positional), call the team, and prepare for emergency caesarean — the goal is to relieve cord compression until delivery.
keyClue: Pulsating cord palpated below the presenting part with variable decelerations — cord prolapse requiring immediate manual elevation
commonExamTrap: Attempting to replace the cord into the uterus, which risks vasospasm — it should be kept moist but not repositioned.
missedConcept: Manual elevation of the presenting part is the single most effective immediate action to relieve cord compression in cord prolapse.
redFlagReason: Cord prolapse is an immediately life-threatening emergency; untreated cord compression causes fetal hypoxia, acidosis, and death within minutes.

clinicalStem: An obstetric team is reviewing cases of cord prolapse on their unit over the last 3 years as part of a quality improvement audit. They identify that the majority of cases occurred in specific clinical scenarios. A 30-year-old woman at 37 weeks is currently admitted with footling breech presentation. Artificial rupture of membranes (ARM) is being considered to augment slow labour. Which combination of factors in this patient places her at the highest cumulative risk of cord prolapse?
options: Term gestation with cephalic presentation and intact membranes
Footling breech presentation and plan to perform artificial rupture of membranes
Multigravida status and a single fetus in cephalic presentation
Oligohydramnios with cephalic engagement at the pelvic brim
Previous caesarean section with an anterior low-lying placenta
explanation: Cord prolapse risk is highest when the presenting part is poorly applied to the lower uterine segment, leaving space for the cord to descend. Footling breech is one of the highest-risk presentations (~5% risk) because the irregular foot does not form a seal over the cervix. Artificial rupture of membranes in this setting dramatically increases the risk further, as the cushioning effect of forewaters is lost. Other high-risk features include prematurity, high head/unengaged head, transverse/oblique lie, polyhydramnios, and multiple pregnancy. ARM with footling breech is therefore an extremely high-risk combination and should be avoided without senior obstetric presence and contingency for immediate delivery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cephalic presentation at term with intact membranes is low risk; the well-applied head acts as a plug minimising cord prolapse risk.
Correct — footling breech leaves a large gap between the presenting part and the cervix, and ARM removes the hydrostatic protection of forewaters, making this the highest-risk combination.
Multigravidous cephalic presentation increases slightly due to pendulous abdomen and free-floating head, but is far lower risk than footling breech with planned ARM.
Oligohydramnios typically reduces cord prolapse risk as the cord is less mobile; it is polyhydramnios that increases risk.
Previous caesarean section with low-lying placenta increases risk of placenta praevia and haemorrhage, not specifically cord prolapse.
clinicalPearl: Cord prolapse risk is highest with footling breech, transverse lie, unengaged presenting part, and polyhydramnios — ARM in these settings should only be performed with immediate delivery capability.
keyClue: Footling breech + plan for ARM = highest cumulative risk of cord prolapse
commonExamTrap: Confusing oligohydramnios (reduced risk — cord less mobile) with polyhydramnios (increased risk) as a predisposing factor for cord prolapse.
missedConcept: ARM in malpresentation with a poorly-applied presenting part dramatically increases cord prolapse risk by removing the protective forewaters.

topic: Cord prolapse — prognosis and perinatal outcomes
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks' gestation has an emergency caesarean section for cord prolapse. The cord prolapse was recognised immediately on vaginal examination when she presented with ruptured membranes and the CTG was noted to be normal at the time of discovery. Manual elevation of the presenting part was achieved within 2 minutes. The decision-to-delivery interval (DDI) was 18 minutes. The neonate is delivered with Apgar scores of 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes respectively. Which single factor in this case most strongly correlates with the favourable neonatal outcome?
options: Primigravida status reducing intrapartum complication risk
Gestational age of 38 weeks ensuring fetal lung maturity
Short decision-to-delivery interval with a normal CTG at the time of cord prolapse recognition
Use of general rather than regional anaesthesia allowing faster surgical access
The maternal age of 22 years conferring physiological resilience to the fetus
explanation: Perinatal outcome in cord prolapse is predominantly determined by two factors: the fetal condition (CTG pattern) at the time of diagnosis, and the decision-to-delivery interval (DDI). A normal CTG at recognition indicates no significant acidosis has yet occurred, and a DDI ≤30 minutes is consistently associated with significantly better Apgar scores and reduced hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy. In this case, both favourable prognostic elements are present: normal CTG at recognition + DDI of 18 minutes. Gestational age and parity have minor roles compared with the DDI and initial fetal condition.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Primigravida status does not independently predict neonatal outcome in cord prolapse; parity is not a primary prognostic factor in this emergency.
Fetal lung maturity is important for respiratory distress syndrome risk but is not the primary determinant of outcomes from acute hypoxia in cord prolapse.
Correct — a normal fetal heart rate pattern at diagnosis combined with a short DDI (<30 minutes) are the strongest predictors of good neonatal outcome after cord prolapse.
The choice between general and regional anaesthesia affects DDI but is not independently a prognostic factor; the DDI itself is the variable that matters, regardless of anaesthetic technique.
Maternal age at 22 has no direct physiological effect on fetal resilience to hypoxia in cord prolapse.
clinicalPearl: In cord prolapse, the two strongest predictors of favourable neonatal outcome are a normal CTG at the time of diagnosis and a decision-to-delivery interval of <30 minutes.
keyClue: Normal CTG at cord prolapse recognition + DDI of 18 minutes = favourable neonatal prognosis
commonExamTrap: Attributing good neonatal outcome to gestational age or parity rather than the DDI and initial fetal heart rate pattern, which are the evidence-based prognostic determinants.
missedConcept: Decision-to-delivery interval and fetal condition at diagnosis are the primary modifiable determinants of neonatal outcome in cord prolapse.
